BODY_TEXT: Hi everyone,I’m hoping someone can help me because I’m getting pretty frustrated with my Philips Series 3000 Airfryer.The problem is that the airfryer randomly shuts off completely while I’m using it. It’s not just that the heating stops or that it pauses — the entire device turns off. The display goes completely black, as if it has lost all power.At first, I thought it might be something with the power outlet at home, so I tested it in another outlet. The problem still happened. I also made sure it wasn’t an issue with an extension cable or something similar.Because the problem continued, I brought it back to MediaMarkt, where I bought it. A MediaMarkt employee tested the airfryer in the store himself, and during that test the airfryer also shut off completely. So the problem was not only happening at my home — it was also happening in the store.After that, MediaMarkt sent it to Philips for inspection. Philips checked the airfryer and said they could not find any problem. Because of that, they didn’t repair or replace anything.However, when I got the airfryer back and tested it at home, it still had exactly the same issue. It still randomly turns off completely.The airfryer is around one year old, so it is not an old device. It has been used normally. It is not extremely dirty, and I don’t believe dirt is the cause, because the entire device loses power when it happens.What makes this situation confusing is that the problem has already been confirmed in the store by an employee, but Philips says they cannot find anything wrong with it.I would like to ask:Has anyone else had this problem with a Philips Series 3000 Airfryer?Could this be a power supply issue, overheating protection, or an internal defect?What should I do if Philips says there is no problem, but the airfryer clearly still does not work correctly?Is it reasonable to ask MediaMarkt for a replacement after the first inspection did not solve the issue?I would really appreciate any advice or experiences from people who have had a similar situation.

BODY_TEXT: My Instant Vortex air fryer intermittently will not start after the door is closed. This problem can be caused by the door actuator located at the top center of the door, not actuating the switch on the air fryer body. My solution was to take a small piece of metal tape (adhesive one side) and form it around the actuator only. You may have to add 2 or 3 layers to get it high enough to actuate the switch. Our Vortex Plus is about 4 or 5 years old and I suspect the actuator has worn down enough to loose proprt contact. You can you metal tape used for duct sealing, available at any hardware store. Hope it helps
TITLE: Instant Vortex Plus not working
FULL_TEXT: Instant Vortex Plus not working. My Instant Vortex air fryer intermittently will not start after the door is closed. This problem can be caused by the door actuator located at the top center of the door, not actuating the switch on the air fryer body. My solution was to take a small piece of metal tape (adhesive one side) and form it around the actuator only. You may have to add 2 or 3 layers to get it high enough to actuate the switch. Our Vortex Plus is about 4 or 5 years old and I suspect the actuator has worn down enough to loose proprt contact. You can you metal tape used for duct sealing, available at any hardware store. Hope it helps

BODY_TEXT: Hi all, I’m looking for an air purifier that will help with wildfire smoke. I tend to get significant lung and throat irritation during the wildfire season, which seems to be becoming a several months per year thing now. I’m located in Canada and hoping for something under $300 CAD for the initial purchase. Ideally, the filters are easily available in Canada and won’t cost a fortune to replace over time (>$100/year). My room is 15.4 m2 (~165 sqft). I was looking at the Coway AirMega Mighty 1512HH or the Levoit Vital 200S, but I’m concerned about the cost/availability of replacement filters in Canada, and energy costs if I’m running them on the highest setting for an extended period (the poor AQI seems to last several days to weeks at a time). I considered making a CR box, but they seem to take up a lot of space, are unsightly, and I’m worried they will cost more (I spent $80 on 2 MERV 14 filters at Home Depot today for my home furnace). BODY_TEXT: So i was just sold a new Kirby vacuum. It too a while to remember and recognize the company. Turns out my Mother in law has one and loves hers even after having it for over a decade. However im being told that they are like an MLM and the way they pressure their sells red on commission only is problematic. My experience with the sales reps I had today and the demo I got went so well they had me immediately. I also have a scrappy ryobi vacuum, so it was likely very easy to sell to me. After already being sold and going through the process of buying this vacuum, I asked if they would be cool if I just spread my support for them on my platform as a vtuber. Like some sort of sponsorship. They then said they would give me $100 for every vacuum I sold through my platform which was way more than I was gonna ask for. Honestly I dont really have views and have a small community im still building. Honestly right now just getting my views up is all I really need. Now im being told that I probably shouldn't due to there high pressure methods and how past employees disliked the high pressure and commission only fees. Im conflicted now because I understand the struggles of being a sales reps and retail and I also like the product I got. At the same time if the company is actually corrupt or problematic, i dont want yo support something that could be harmful. I dont feel like I've been scammed or pressured into this, but apparently there are others who have.

BODY_TEXT: After 7 or 8 years of daily use my Dyson animal 2 (UP20? I think), I think its time to retire it. There is some incredibly difficult to pin down high pitched squeaking when on carpet (I think its the brush bar, I tore it down and cleaned it, resolved it for a bit but 6 months later its back) and its served its purpose. I hate getting rid of stuff but I am tired of the screws into plastic and generally poor serviceability on it. I still have been able to but its tedious. I wouldn't say I am unhappy with the dyson (it's easy to do basic cleaning of the machine, I like not having to deal with bags and it seems to clean well enough) but even another dyson like this is $300+ which isn't a ton of money but its enough to take a beat to consider what to buy I think. I got this one for $200 new in box on craigslist and it's been worth that I think.

BODY_TEXT: Every new robot vacuum seems to introduce a "disruptive" feature—better obstacle-crossing ability, higher mopping temperature, AI cameras, robotic arms… every brand is vying to showcase new features. BODY_TEXT: I’ve been following the latest news from Australia: According to reports, a 25-year-old man suffered severe burns after a residential fire was caused by a malfunctioning robot vacuum cleaner. First of all, I sincerely hope he makes a full recovery.As far as I know, the exact cause is still under investigation. It could be the battery, the charging system, the internal wiring, or something else entirely, so I think it’s too early to draw any conclusions.What I’ve been thinking about is this: the greatest danger may not be the robot itself “exploding,” but rather what would happen if an electrical malfunction caused a fire that spread to everything around it.I don’t think anyone needs to panic over this, because electrical safety deserves more attention than the safety of the robot vacuum’s battery itself. For example, we should regularly check the battery for signs of swelling, overheating, or strange odors, and make sure to use the original charging equipment, avoiding cheap replacement parts.Has this incident made you think more deeply about how to use robot vacuums safely? Or has it made you more cautious about electrical safety in your home?

BODY_TEXT: I was testing how different robot vacuums handled coffee grounds in floor gaps today, and something caught my attention. On open floors, almost every robot picked them up without much trouble. But once the coffee grounds settled into the gaps, the results started looking completely different. A few robots drove over the same spot multiple times and still left quite a bit behind. Others pulled most of the debris out on the first or second pass—even though they weren't the ones with the highest advertised suction.

BODY_TEXT: I had an old MOVA S10 Plus a few years back, 7,000pa suction, no wash station etc, no camera for obstacle avoidance (so it sucked in that regard) but it cleaned very quickly. Fast forward today with the Dreame L50 Ultra I've had it for half a year. The suction more than doubled to 20,000pa it's a great vacuum and it does its job very well but it's just slow? It moves slowly, vacuums slowly, it's very very cautious. It's obstacle avoidance is fantastic of course it hasn't snagged a cable or pet toy during my time of owning it. But when it comes across an obstacle it'll stop, back up slowly, then slowly move around, turn it's body around to look at it, move back, move forward and repeat. Does it move slowly to avoid getting trapped on obstacles or is it something else? Because even in a room with no obstacles (carpeted) it moves slowly and yes the carpet deep clean, slower movement settings have been turned off. Not trying to complain because it is a fantastic vacuum but does anyone know why it's so slow?

BODY_TEXT: I got a Samsung 2-in-1 front-loading washing machine / dryer 9 months ago. We would use Tide pods to do laundry. In fact, the machine literally has a pre-programmed Tide pod cycle that users can choose. A couple of weeks ago, the machine leaked within 5 minutes of the start of the cycle. The leak was significant enough that water was dripping through a light fixture downstairs. Despite loading the Tide pod underneath the clothing, there was evidence of plastic residue on the door gasket. Since the machine is less than 1 year old, I put in a warranty claim for A) the machine itself, and B) any damage to the electrical around the light fixture.
TITLE: Using laundry pods negates washing machine warranty
FULL_TEXT: Using laundry pods negates washing machine warranty. I got a Samsung 2-in-1 front-loading washing machine / dryer 9 months ago. We would use Tide pods to do laundry. In fact, the machine literally has a pre-programmed Tide pod cycle that users can choose. A couple of weeks ago, the machine leaked within 5 minutes of the start of the cycle. The leak was significant enough that water was dripping through a light fixture downstairs. Despite loading the Tide pod underneath the clothing, there was evidence of plastic residue on the door gasket. Since the machine is less than 1 year old, I put in a warranty claim for A) the machine itself, and B) any damage to the electrical around the light fixture.
